coredata.dc:title: 6-Glycosylaminoquinoline-assisted LDI MS for detection and imaging of small molecules with enhanced detection selectivity and sensitivity
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Ge
Zhao, Xiaoyong
Guo, Mengzhe
Liu, Yaqin
Shi, Keqiang
Guo, Cheng
Pan, Yuanjiang
coredata.dc:description: Matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization (MALDI) mass spectrometry (MS) is a powerful tool in the analysis and imaging of small molecules. However, MALDI MS analysis is easily subjected to poor signal reproducibility and selectivity, especially for complex samples. In this study, a matrix glycosylation strategy was proposed to synthesize glycosylated matrices with excellent performances by enhancing the interaction of the matrix with small molecules. A series of glycosylated matrices including 3-glycosylaminoquinoline (3-GAQ), 6-glycosylaminoquinoline (6-GAQ), and 1-amino-5-glycosylaminoquinoline (GDAN) were synthesized by connecting glucose with the existing amine matrices. Compared with their parent matrices and the existing matrix (1,5-diaminonaphathelene, 1,5-DAN), the glycosylated matrices exhibited remarkably-improved sensitivity, higher signal reproducibility (RSD < 9%) in detecting metabolites, demonstrating the effectiveness of the glycosylation strategy. Among them, 6-GAQ exhibited the best performance. Using 6-GAQ, the detection limit of citric acid reached the low fmol range, and the calibration curve of citric acid had ideal linearity (R2 > 0.99), proving that 6-GAQ was capable of accurate quantitative analysis of metabolites. Furthermore, 6-GAQ was used for the imaging of metabolites in the mouse kidney section, showing higher sensitivity and lower background noise than the commonly-used matrices, 9-aminoquinoline (9-AA), and 1,5-DAN. More importantly, 6-GAQ can selectively detect the hydrophilic metabolites, especially the hydrophilic lipids in the mouse kidney. Overall, 6-GAQ is an ideal matrix potentially applied in the imaging and quantitative analysis of hydrophilic small molecules in complex samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Glycosylated matrix
Metabolite detection
Quantitative analysis
Matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization (MALDI)
Mass spectrometry imaging (MSI)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of matrix and standard solutions
Preparation of tissue sections for MSI
MALDI MS, MSI and MS/MS analysis
High resolution LC-MS/MS for metabolites identification
Synthesis and characterization of glycosylated matrix
Detection of metabolites by glycosylated matrix
6-GAQ-assisted LDI MS imaging of mouse kidneys

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of the glycosylated matrices.

MALDI mass spectra of metabolite mixtures in negative ion mode using 3AQ, 6AQ, DAN (A) and the glycosylated matrices (B) respectively. 1, pyruvate; 2, taurine; 3, aspartate; 4, malate; 5, glutamate; 6, glutamine; 7, citrate; 8, glutathione.

MALDI MS spectra of 20 amino acids (A), peptide mixture containing BGP Gla Protein (45–49), Nocistatin, ACTH (4–10), ACTH (4–11), Neurokinin B (B) fatty acid mixture containing FA(12:0), FA(16:0), FA(18:0), FA(18:1) and FA(20:4) (C), detected by 6-GAQ matrix in the negative ion mode.

(A) Cocrystallization morphologies and (B) variation of relative intensity of citric acid to tricarballylic acid (IS) acquired from 30 different positions of the sample spots using the matrix 3AQ (a), 6AQ (b), DAN (c), 3-GAQ (d), 6-GAQ (e) and GDAN (f) respectively.

RSD values of the signal intensities of citric acid and malic acid over the sample spot (a), and the MSI images of citric acid using 6-GAQ (b), and the calibration curves of citric acid (c), and malic acid (d) using 6-GAQ as matrix.

MALDI mass spectra acquired from the matrices (A) and the kidney section (B) using 9AA, DAN and 6-GAQ as matrix respectively in negative ion mode. Asterisk represents matrix interference, and the insertion represents enlarged mass spectra from m/z 60 to 350 in Fig. 6B.

Spatial distributions of various metabolites observed in three mice kidneys in negative ion mode. The step size is 200 μm for mouse 1 and 2, 400 μm for mouse 3. The above images were obtained from three db/m mice at 16 weeks old.
Physicochemical properties of the standard matrices and glycosylated matrices.

head.ce:title: 6-Glycosylaminoquinoline-assisted LDI MS for detection and imaging of small molecules with enhanced detection selectivity and sensitivity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou, 310027, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou, 310027, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of New Drug Research and Clinical Pharmacy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of New Drug Research and Clinical Pharmacy, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou, Jiangsu, 221004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cancer Institute (Key Laboratory of Cancer Prevention and Intervention, China National Ministry of Education), The Second Affiliated Hospital, Zhejiang University School of Medicine, Hangzhou, Zhejiang, 310009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Cancer Institute (Key Laboratory of Cancer Prevention and Intervention

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Cancer Institute (Key Laboratory of Cancer Prevention and Intervention, China National Ministry of Education), The Second Affiliated Hospital, Zhejiang University School of Medicine, Hangzhou, Zhejiang, 310009, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization (MALDI) mass spectrometry (MS) is a powerful tool in the analysis and imaging of small molecules. However, MALDI MS analysis is easily subjected to poor signal reproducibility and selectivity, especially for complex samples. In this study, a matrix glycosylation strategy was proposed to synthesize glycosylated matrices with excellent performances by enhancing the interaction of the matrix with small molecules. A series of glycosylated matrices including 3-glycosylaminoquinoline (3-GAQ), 6-glycosylaminoquinoline (6-GAQ), and 1-amino-5-glycosylaminoquinoline (GDAN) were synthesized by connecting glucose with the existing amine matrices. Compared with their parent matrices and the existing matrix (1,5-diaminonaphathelene, 1,5-DAN), the glycosylated matrices exhibited remarkably-improved sensitivity, higher signal reproducibility (RSD < 9%) in detecting metabolites, demonstrating the effectiveness of the glycosylation strategy. Among them, 6-GAQ exhibited the best performance. Using 6-GAQ, the detection limit of citric acid reached the low fmol range, and the calibration curve of citric acid had ideal linearity (R2 > 0.99), proving that 6-GAQ was capable of accurate quantitative analysis of metabolites. Furthermore, 6-GAQ was used for the imaging of metabolites in the mouse kidney section, showing higher sensitivity and lower background noise than the commonly-used matrices, 9-aminoquinoline (9-AA), and 1,5-DAN. More importantly, 6-GAQ can selectively detect the hydrophilic metabolites, especially the hydrophilic lipids in the mouse kidney. Overall, 6-GAQ is an ideal matrix potentially applied in the imaging and quantitative analysis of hydrophilic small molecules in complex samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Matrix assisted-laser desorption/ionization (MALDI) mass spectrometry (MS) is a powerful tool in the analysis of high molecular weight (bio)molecules, such as proteomics [1–4], glycomics [5–7], interaction of proteins with other substrates [8,9], and microbial identification [10–13]. Recently, the application of MALDI MS to small molecules is continuously increasing, driven by the growing interest in small molecules (for instance, metabolites [14–16], drugs [17,18], and contaminants [19,20]), and by the overwhelming growth of mass spectrometry imaging (MSI) techniques [21,22]. The successful application of MALDI MS to small molecules analysis is strictly dependent on the choice of the matrix that should meet specific requirements, including homogeneous cocrystallization of matrix and analytes, efficient analyte ionization, and null or minimal interference in the low mass range of mass spectra (usually m/z < 500) [23].

Various MALDI matrices have been explored for small molecules analysis. Organic matrices such as 1,5-diaminonaphthalene (DAN) [24] and N-(1-naphthyl) ethylenediamine dihydrochloride (NEDC) [25] and various inorganic materials [26,27] like porous silicon [28,29], carbon materials [30–33] and metal nanoparticles [34,35] were reported to be the low-background matrices that produce null or minimal interference to analytes. 9-Aminoquinoline (9AA) [36], 2,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid (DHB) and (2-[(2E)-3-(4-tert-butylphenyl)-2-methylprop-2-enylidene]malononitrile) (DCTB) [37] have good sensitivity and broad molecular coverage in MALDI imaging of metabolites in biological tissues, but they lead to inhomogeneous cocrystallization with analytes, limiting their application to MALDI quantitative analysis. Recently, the fabrication of thin-film materials as effective substrates for surface-assisted laser desorption/ionization (SALDI) has attracted immense interest mainly due to the enhancement of sensitivity and reproducibility. For example, two-dimensional multilayered gold-nanoparticle film [38], silicon nanopost array chip [39], metal-organic framework [40], and functionalized porous TiO2 [34] film perform well in MALDI quantitative analysis and imaging. However, the fabrication and synthesis of the substrates are usually time-consuming and complicated. What's more, it's a challenge to realize the selectivity of matrix for detecting small molecules in complex samples. Since MALDI MS suffers from severe ion suppression in the analysis of complex samples [35], achieving matrix selectivity could alleviate the problem by detecting targeted ions.

In this study, a matrix glycosylation strategy was proposed to improve the sensitivity, reproducibility and selectivity of small molecules analysis by enhancing the matrix interaction with small molecules. Glycosylated matrices including 3-glycosylaminoquiniline (3-GAQ), 6-glycosylaminoquinoline (6-GAQ), and 1-amino-5-glycosylaminoquinoline (GDAN) were easily synthesized by connecting glucose with the existing amine matrices. Compared with their parent matrices and the existing matrix (DAN and 9AA), the glycosylated matrices exhibited better performance in the detection of small molecules with remarkably-improved sensitivity, and higher reproducibility. More importantly, 6-GAQ showed good selectivity for hydrophilic metabolites detection. 6-GAQ was applicable in accurate quantitative analysis and imaging of metabolites by MALDI MS.

1,5-Diaminonaphathelene (DAN), 9-aminoacridine (9AA), 6-aminoquinoline (6AQ), 3-aminoquinoline (3AQ), glucose, acetic acid, and standards including pyruvate, taurine, aspartate, malate, glutamate, glutamine, citrate, glutathione, amino acids standards, fatty acids standards and peptide standards used in the article were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). LC-MS grade methanol, acetonitrile and ethanol were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Deionized water was prepared by Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore, Billerica, MA, USA).

The mice used in this study were provided by Nanjing biomedical research institute of Nanjing University (Nanjing, China). Three male db/m mice were kept in an environmentally controlled room (20–24 °C, 40–70% humidity) and fed a standard diet and water until 16 weeks old. Mice were anesthetized with isoflurane prior to sacrificing by neck fracture. Kidneys were removed and flash-frozen in liquid nitrogen and stored at −80 °C for MSI analysis. The animal experiments were performed according to the NIH Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals (National Institutes of Health Publications, No. 3040-2, revised 1999, Bethesda, MD) and approved by the Animal Care and Use Committee in Nanjing, China.

Glycosylated matrices were synthesized according to the published procedures [41]. To 5 mL of aqueous solution of glucose (0.90 g, 5 mmol, 1.0 equiv), 5 mL ethanol solutions of the amine (e.g. 6AQ, 3AQ or DAN, 10 mmol, 2.0 equiv) were added as well as 2–3 drops of acetic acid. The reactants were reacted by continuously stirring for 3 h at 60 °C under reflux. After reaction, the solvent of reaction mixtures was removed by vacuo, and the residues were purified by recrystallization in ethanol to afford the pure glycosylated matrices, characterized by NMR and HRMS (See the supporting information).
The physiochemical properties (UV absorption, proton affinities and logP) of matrices were measured and calculated as described in the supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of matrix and standard solutions
ce:section.ce:para: The matrices 9AA, DAN, 3AQ, 6AQ were dissolved into ethanol at the concentration of 10 mg mL−1 respectively. The glycosylated matrices 6-GAQ, 3-GAQ and GDAN were dissolved in 50% ethanol/water at the concentration of 10 mg mL−1. Stock solutions of small molecules (pyruvate, taurine, aspartate, malate, glutamate, glutamine, citrate, glutathione, and amino acids) were prepared in water at 10 mM, respectively. Fatty acids used in this article were prepared in acetonitrile at 10 mM. All stock solutions were kept at 4 °C before use.

Conventional dried-droplet method was used to prepare samples for MALDI analysis as follows: the standard solution was mixed with the matrix solution at ratio of 1:1 (v/v); 1 μL of the resulting mixture was pipetted on the ground steel MALDI target, followed by drying under fume hood.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of tissue sections for MSI
ce:section.ce:para: Mice kidneys were cryosectioned at −16 °C into 14 μm thick slices in a Leica CM1950 cryostat (Leica Microsystems GmbH, Wetzlar, Germany) and thaw mounted onto indium tin oxide (ITO) coated glass slides (Bruker Daltonics). The glass slides were dried in a vacuum desiccator for approximately 30 min. Next, 6-GAQ solution at 10 mg mL−1 was sprayed onto the tissue sections using an automatic matrix sprayer (ImagePrep, Bruker Daltonics) to form a homogeneous coverage of matrix over the entire tissue surface. The tissue sections were then dried for another 30 min before MSI analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MALDI MS, MSI and MS/MS analysis
ce:section.ce:para: MALDI MS analysis was performed on ultrafleXtreme MALDI-TOF/TOF MS (Bruker Daltonics, Billerica, MA) equipped with a Smartbeam Nd:YAG 355 nm laser. The sample analysis was operated in negative reflectron mode with pulsed ion extraction time of 100 ns, accelerating voltage of 19.98 kV, extraction voltage of 17.88 kV, lens voltage of 6.01 kV, reflector voltage of 21.1 kV. The laser was fired at a repetition rate of 1000 Hz, and set as “Ultra focus” with a ∼100 μm diameter footprint. The laser intensity was optimized prior to each run and then fixed for the whole experiment. The mass spectrum was acquired over a mass range of m/z 40–1000 Da. Mass calibration was performed with external standards prior to data acquisition. The negative MS/MS fragmentations was performed using the LIFT technique with 10–20% higher laser intensity compared with the MS mode. The reflector voltage was also maximized to 29.55 kV. Other parameters were set: accelerating voltage 7.5 kV, extraction voltage 6.75 kV, lens voltage 3.51 kV, LIFT 1, voltage 18.98 kV, LIFT 2 voltage 4.20 kV, mass isolation range 2 Da. The MS/MS mass spectra were calibrated by peptide standard mixtures before data acquisition. Lipids detected in the mouse kidney were identified by analyzing fragmentation ions in tandem mass spectra and referring to the LIPID MAPS (http://www.lipidmaps.org/) and Human Metabolome Database (http://www.hmdb.ca/).

The instrument parameters for MSI were set: 500 laser shots, laser intensity of 65%, spatial resolution of 200 μm for the kidney imaging. MALDI mass spectra were normalized using the total ion current (TIC) method [42]. The signal intensity of each imaging data represented the normalized intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: High resolution LC-MS/MS for metabolites identification
ce:section.ce:para: To further identify metabolites in the mouse kidney, LC-MS/MS was used to analyze the kidney homogenate. The mouse kidney weighted about 100 mg was placed into a 1.5 mL centrifuge tube and mixed with 0.5 mL of 80% methanol aqueous solution. The homogenization was performed for 5 min at an electric homogenizer (MY-20, Jingxin, Shanghai). The mixture was centrifuged for 15 min to obtain the supernatant, and the precipitate was ultrasonic extracted by chloroform/methanol (1:1, v/v) three times. Then, the two parts of supernatants were combined for nitrogen drying. The final residue was weighted approximately 10 mg and stored at −20 °C for LC-MS analysis.

Accurate mass measurement and MS/MS data acquisition of metabolites in kidney homogenate was performed on LC MS IT-TOF mass spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) equipped with an electrospray ionization (ESI) source. A 10 μL aliquot of the mouse kidney homogenate was injected into the LC-MS system with gradient separation condition of 0–60 min, 10–90% methanol/water. The C18 chromatographic column (Shiseido, Japan) has a 250 mm length, 4.6 mm dimension and 5 μm particle size. The MS parameters were set as follows: ion source voltage −3.0 kV, detector voltage 1.57 kV, nebulizing gas (nitrogen) flow 1.5 L/min, dry gas flow 100 kPa, collision energy set as 30% for MS2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of glycosylated matrix
ce:section.ce:para: In the MALDI ionization mechanism, analyte is wrapped into matrix crystals, and the formation of co-crystals between the matrix and the analyte is a key factor affecting the energy transfer and proton transfer between matrix and analytes [43,44]. The high chemical affinity and compatibility between the matrix and the analyte can promote the co-crystallization formation [45]. For example, Tanaka, K. et al. synthesized a novel matrix additive o-alkylated dihydroxybenzoic acid (ADHB) to increase the hydrophobicity of the conventional 2,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid (DHB), and thus enhancing its affinity towards hydrophobic peptides and improve the sensitivity [46]. Hercules, D.M. et al. used fructose as a matrix additive to DHB to increase the hydrophobic protein/matrix interactions and optimize protein inclusion in the matrix crystals, resulting in the significant enhancement in spectral reproducibility, signal intensity, and mass resolution [47].

Glucose is a hydrophilic and polar compound with rich hydroxyls, which easily form hydrogen bonds and interact with hydrophilic compounds. We presumed that covalently binding glucose to the existing matrix to synthesize a glycosylated matrix could improve the hydrophilicity of the matrix and its affinity to hydrophilic analytes, and hence facilitate the formation of matrix/analyte cocrystallizaiton.

As shown in Fig. 1 , glucose was covalently bound with three amine matrices (6AQ, 3AQ and DAN) to synthesize 6-glycosylaminoquinoline (6-GAQ), 3-glycosylaminoquinoline (3-GAQ), and glycosyl-1,5-diaminonaphathelene (GDAN).

The physicochemical properties including extinction coefficients at 355 nm (ε 355nm), proton affinity (PA), and lipophilicity (logP) have an effect on matrix performance. The ε 355nm of matrix implies the absorption ability of matrix to the MALDI laser [48]. PA is an indicator of gas-phase basicity of matrix, which affects proton transfer between matrix and analytes [49]. LogP is a parameter of the polarity of compounds. As listed in Table 1 , the ε 355nm and PA of 3-GAQ, 6-GAQ and GDAN were changed a little, compared with their parent matrices. In addition, compared with the widely-used matrix DAN [24], the glycosylated matrices had higher values of ε 355nm and PA, indicating that the glycosylated matrices might have better performance in assisting analyte desorption/ionization in negative ion mode. logP of the glycosylated matrices was sharply-decreased, suggesting the enhancement of water solubility of glycosylated matrices. Actually, the glycosylated matrices were soluble in water at 10 mg mL−1, but their parent matrices were slightly water-soluble (0–0.4 mg mL−1). The glucose moiety of matrix enhanced the interaction and compatibility between matrix and hydrophilic analytes. Therefore, the matrix with glucose group might have an effect on the detection sensitivity, reproducibility and selectivity of small molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of metabolites by glycosylated matrix
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the effect of glycosylation on the detection sensitivity, metabolites mixtures including pyruvate, taurine, aspartate, malate, glutamate, glutamine, citrate and glutathione were analyzed. As shown in Fig. 2 , 3-GAQ, 6-GAQ and GDAN could detect all the standards with higher ion intensities, while the parent matrices could only detect some of the metabolites, demonstrating that the sensitivity was improved by matrix glycosylation. Compared with the existing matrix DAN, the glycosylated matrix detected the metabolites with better sensitivity. 6-GAQ was screened out among the glycosylated matrices because it detected metabolites with the highest sensitivity. Then, various standards including 20 amino acids, 5 fatty acids, 5 small peptides were detected by 6-GAQ to test the broad applicability of 6-GAQ. As shown in Fig. 3 , small peptides and most of amino acids with hydrophilic R group (Gln, Asn, Arg, Cys, Thr and Ser) had intense signals in the mass spectra. Amino acids with hydrophobic R group (Trp, Ala and Phe) and the fatty acids had relatively lower intensities, indicating that 6-GAQ could detect hydrophilic metabolites with good selectivity.

The cocrystallization morphology of analytes/matrix was also characterized. The cocrystallization images in Fig. 4 A showed that 3-AQ, 6-AQ and DAN formed irregular and inhomogeneous cocrystallization morphology on the sample spots, while the glycosylated matrices formed a much more homogeneous thin layer film. Scan electron microscopy was used to observe the cocrystallization size and micro morphology. From the images in Fig. S1, we could also see 6-AQ formed inconsistent and irreproducible crystal structures, while 6-GAQ didn't produce any crystals but formed thin layer film. In other words, the glycosylation changed the cocrystallization morphology of matrix from solid crystals to transparent thin film.

To further investigate the possible improvements in position-to-position reproducibility using the glycosylated matrices, the glycosylated matrices and the amine matrices were used to detect citric acid in 30 different positions of the sample spots. A concentration of 250 pmol of tricarballylic acid was used as internal standard (IS), and the signal intensity ratio of analyte to IS was used as relative intensity to investigate the reproducibility. Using the parent matrices, relative standard deviations (RSD) of citric acid ranged from 14.9 to 27% (Fig. 4B). Employing the glycosylated matrices, the RSD of citric acid was down to 7.4–9.0%. In Fig. 4B, the relative intensity of analyte to IS resulted from the glycosylated matrix was lower than the parent matrices, mainly because the IS (tricarballylic acid) had much more prominent signal intensity using the glycosylated matrices. From the above results, we concluded that the matrix glycosylation could significantly improve the detection sensitivity, cocrystallization morphology homogeneity, and signal reproducibility of small molecules.

We chose 6-GAQ to prove its potential in accurate quantification of metabolites by MALDI MS. The spot-to-spot reproducibility was characterized by measuring RSD of malic acid and citric acid spotted on the stainless target with five replicates. The RSDs were both less than 6% with 6-GAQ, however, exceeding 12% with the existing matrix 9-AA (Fig. 5 a). The distribution of citric acid in the 6-GAQ sample spot was analyzed by MALDI imaging (Fig. 5b). Citric acid was found to distribute uniformly over the entire sample spot. Internal standard method was used to construct the calibration curves of citric acid and malic acid. A concentration of 250 pmol of tricarballylic acid was used as IS, and it had no negative effect on the MS analysis. The signal intensity ratio of analyte to IS was used as the response, and each concentration was detected with triplicates. Using 6-GAQ, the best linear response was attained at the concentration range from 200 to 3200 pmol (equally 0.4–6.4 mM) for citric acid and from 25 to 800 pmol for malic acid, respectively (Fig. 5c&d). Coefficients (R2) of the calibration curves were both more than 0.99, demonstrating the good linearity with 6-GAQ matrix. The limit of detection (LOD) was measured for the analyte ions by laser ablation of a tiny spot to obtain a spectrum in which the analyte signal to noise ratio was above 3. The LODs of citric acid and malic acid were measured to be 500 and 50 fmol, corresponding to 1 and 0.1 μM, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 6-GAQ-assisted LDI MS imaging of mouse kidneys
ce:section.ce:para: Considering that small molecules detection is easily interfered by matrix in the low m/z range [50], we tested the matrix-related peaks of 6-GAQ and the conventional matrices (9-AA and DAN). As shown in Fig. 6 A, 6-GAQ produced cleaner spectra with fewer background peaks in the low mass range, compared with 9-AA and DAN. For example, 9-AA produced a peak of m/z 96.95, which would cause interference to the metabolite phosphoric acid (m/z 96.97). DAN produced more matrix-related peaks, among which m/z 112.97 would overlap with the metabolite thiosulfate (m/z 112.93). Besides the endogenous metabolites, exogeneous compounds such as drugs and pollutants at those m/z will be also interfered by the matrix when they are analyzed. Matrix interference not only affects accuracy of metabolite qualitative and quantitative analysis, but also makes an error in imaging of metabolite distribution.

Then, the matrices were used for in-situ analysis of the mouse kidney. 6-GAQ detected much more metabolite signals especially lipids than 9AA and DAN (Fig. 6B). Endogenous metabolites in the kidney detected by 6-GAQ were identified through accurate mass measurement by LCMS IT-TOF and MALDI-MS/MS analysis as well as the search of LIPIDMAPS database. 52 metabolites including carboxylic acids, amino acids, nucleotides, and lipids as well as redox-related metabolites were identified (Table S1&S2 and Fig. S2). As an example in Fig. S2, MS/MS of m/z 778.5 corresponding to (3'sulfo) Galβ-Cer (d18:1/16:0) produced a characteristic fragment ion at m/z 97 (sulfuric acid group) and 241 (sulfatides head group).

Notably, we detected strong signals of the hydrophilic sulfogalactoceramide (SM4s), but didn't detect out phosphatidylcholine (PC) and phosphatidylethanolamine (PE) from the mice kidney (Table S1). As we know, the content of lipophilic lipid such as PC and PE is much more abundant than that of hydrophilic lipid (sulfatides) in kidneys [51,52]. The phenomenon further proved that 6-GAQ had a selective detection for hydrophilic metabolites.

Before imaging, vacuum stability of 6-GAQ was evaluated by monitoring the alterations in both of tissue weight and signal intensities within 16 h in MALDI ion source (Fig. S3). The signal intensities and tissue weight with RSD below 20% were hardly fluctuated within 16 h, demonstrating the high stability of 6-GAQ under high-vacuum conditions. Then, 6-GAQ was applied to MALDI imaging of metabolites in the mouse kidney. We set the step size of MALDI imaging as 200 and 400 μm, not only for saving energy, but also capable of visualizing the kidney morphology (e.g. renal cortex, medulla and pelvis). The images of representative ions from the kidney were presented in Fig. 7 . MSI of kidney sections reveled several representative ions of the different morphological regions of the organ. For example, m/z 173 (arginine) was mainly found in the renal pelvis, and AMP and FA (16:0) were found in renal cortex, and the lipid SM4s (d18:1/h20:0) were mainly distributed in renal medulla, the distributions of which were similar to the literatures reported [53,54]. Three kidneys collected from three db/m mice at 16 weeks old were analyzed by MALDI MSI. As shown in Fig. 7, the distribution patterns of selected metabolites were similar to each other. The images of other metabolites were exhibited in Fig. S4. From the imaging results, 6-GAQ can be considered to be a good matrix with good selectivity, broad molecular coverage and low background, applicable to MALDI imaging of tissue-specific metabolite distributions.

In summary, a matrix glycosylation strategy was demonstrated to improve the detection reproducibility, selectivity and sensitivity of small molecules by enhancing the interaction between matrix and analytes. A series of glycosylated matrices including 3-glycosylaminoquiniline (3-GAQ), 6-glycosylaminoquinoline (6-GAQ), and 1-amino-5-glycosylaminoquinoline (GDAN) were synthesized and characterized by their physicochemical properties. Compared with their parent matrices and the existing matrix DAN and 9AA, the glycosylated matrices exhibited better performance in detecting metabolites with remarkably-improved sensitivity, higher reproducibility (RSD < 9%). What's more, 6-GAQ showed good selectivity for hydrophilic metabolites detection. Our study indicates that the interaction between matrix and analytes has an effect on matrix performances which is helpful in matrix design for selective detection of a class of molecules.

In clinical and biological analysis, MALDI-MS-based quantification offers a faster and more cost-effective alternative to LC-ESI-MS, and MALDI-MS can also provide spatial distributions of molecules in tissues. The excellent properties of 6-GAQ in hydrophilic metabolites detection make it an ideal matrix for rapid quantification of metabolites and in-situ molecular imaging of biological tissues.

Ge Ma: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xiaoyong Zhao: Validation, Writing – review & editing. Mengzhe Guo: Resources, Investigation. Keqiang Shi: Investigation. Cheng Guo: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Yuanjiang Pan: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (21532005, 21402172, and 22176167), the China State Key Research (2016YFF0200503) and Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (LY19B050007) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2022.339620.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Aebersold, M. Mann, Mass spectrometry-based proteomics, Nature, 422 (2003) 198-207.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel MALDI LIFT-TOF/TOF mass spectrometer for proteomics
